speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to pick it up with your travels en route to Italy. You said you went through Yugoslavia--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungary. We went first-- where did we went? Austria we went. We went first to Hungary I think. We went to Hungary then to Austria because we crossed the Alps. It was unlegal, you know. We have to-- We went-- That was from the Zionist organization was organized. And I was carrying the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby was-- that time, was maybe three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your oldest son, Sheldon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sheldon, yeah. And we, we stayed in, in, in a special place. It was maybe 50 people in one room. And we were sitting there, and I was out of diapers, you know. And I remember I made him a little dress. I sew him a little dress, black and white polka dots for the little boy. And I was playing with him, and then a lot of Hungarian Jews came to see sometime, you know-- somebody from their family because it was like a transitional place. And, you know, it was very rare to see a Jewish child at that time. And he was such a beautiful child. And, and one of them who came said, let me hold him a little.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took him, and then he peed on him. He didn't-- I didn't have no diapers or nothing. And he said, my God! A Jewish boy, and he's not circumcised. I said, who could I circumcise him? Because when I had him in Rowne, they had to circumcise him. And the night before, the person who circumcised ran away because the Russian want to arrest him. And there was-- couldn't do it. So we figured he'll never be-- go to our destination. If we settle down, we will do it. So I travelled, and he saw what happened. He said, my God. He's going to be circumcised.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had two doctors in between the people together. And I said, I wouldn't let you do nothing, because I don't know how long we going to stay. And sometime it's complication. I said, don't bother. He said, leave it to me. The next morning, we were still asleep. He came with a rabbi with such a beard-- that Hungarian that [INAUDIBLE]. And he said, we're going to circumcise the baby. I said, no. You're not going to do it. He grabbed the baby until my husband got dressed, they circumcised him. Took him to another room, and they circumcises. And there was a family who, who survived from a bunker from Poland. You know, a husband and a wife, and they have two children. So they were the one who kept the baby. He said, you going to see everything will fall in place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, like I said, till my husband put on the pants and ran the hall, it was done. And you know, looking, there was one more boy who was born in the bunker. And he was six months old. So the parents begged the same rabbi, and he circumcised them. And the doctors was cleaning in case something happened. He said, nothing will happen. You know, he--

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there for one week, and that rabbi who circumcise him and that man came twice a day with a pail they used to bathe them. And nothing happened. It healed. Nothing-- to both of the children, nothing. And then from Hungary, we went to Yugoslavia. And in Yugoslavia, we went like Greeks-- you know, who was in charge said we are Greeks from the concentration camp, and we want to go home. So they let us in Yugoslavia. And while we were in Yugoslavia, we were in Belgrade. So they went there to the authorities, and he said we couldn't go to, to Bulgaria because Bulgaria's going to have a fight with Yugoslavia. You know, that wasn't true. They made up that. So they gave us papers to go to Italy. And that's the way we went to the Mediterranean.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Italy, and with us we took two Jewish people who had very big positions in Yugoslavia. And they run away from there. So one was sitting next to me, and I kept the baby on him. And then the Russian came to, to check. They thought he's the husband. You see? My husband was sitting the opposite place. So we, we took them out, two of them. And we came to Trieste, and we stayed there a couple weeks. And from Trieste, we went to Florence. And from Florence, we went to Rome. And on the skirts on the Rome, they had-- they made settlements for the Jewish refugees. And then there was-- a, a matter of where you want to go? My husband want to go to Israel because he had a brother there. And people who want to go America were in a different location. So we were staying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, one time I told you, my husband went with them to the city because the people want to look up relatives in America. And my husband said his relatives live in Milwaukee. And they say that is not such a thing, Milwaukee. You must be crazy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said he wants to go and now. And I told him, look. If everybody said you're crazy, don't bother. You must have mix up something.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he were-- he was persistent. So he went, and he came to the consulate. And before anybody looked in the telephone books, he asked the secretary-- he said, is there a Milwaukee in America. She said, yes. It's a beautiful city.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told them the name, and she looked up a cousin. The cousin originally live in Waukegan, but there was the same name from a man who lived in Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she gave him the name from the man who lives in Chicago, the same [? Berland, ?] Sam [? Berland. ?] So my husband went and wrote him a letter and told him his name, his mother's name. And he's looking for Sam [? Berland. ?] His mother's name was like this and the father like that. And if he is the one, he would like to hear from him. It happened that [? Berland ?] know the cousin in Waukegan. He was a big businessman, too. So he called him right away up. And he right away wrote a letter, and then we had in Green Bay cousins and in Milwaukee. And the family was just wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you change from going to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We want to go to Israel. Because I had a baby, they wouldn't take me. We sent the money what we had. My husband used to do business in Italy. He used to work for butchers. And he was-- used to ask-- when they used to buy a cow, he used to estimate. And they used to pay him very well. He was a very good businessman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we send the clothes and everything, we sent the-- with the people to Israel. And-- but we ourselves couldn't go because at the time, they went unlegally. And instead 50 people, they used to put 200 people. With a baby you couldn't do it. So we couldn't do, but we were waiting. In the meantime, he found out from the relatives-- so the relatives said, right away, if you want to come, we make you papers. So we were debating, you know, what to do. To Israel didn't look we could go so quick. And to America, it didn't look too good neither because the Polish quarter was very used up. But the Russian quarter was not used up. So we had all this-- we made friends. My husband had a lot of friends. So one time we were walking and one said to him, you know, don't tell nobody, but I'm going to Spain. And with Spain, with a cargo ship, I'm going to go to America. And my husband said, how come you could go, you could get a visa. He said, you know, I made I was born in Russia. In the Russia quarter, you could get a visa. He said, why don't you do that? So he said, what shall we do? I said, look it. I was in Russia. I don't even remember the city there, but we had a friend in Russia when we were living there. And she didn't want to go-- we begged her to come with us, but she had a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: She heard she had a sister, but she said she doesn't want to go without the sister. But who know where the sister was. But she was a Russian. She was a Russian. So I got her name, and I got the city. I said, in case something happen, let them find out the city was demolished and how even in Russia, because the Germans were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we wrote to the relatives, and we told them to put the names. So they were very surprised. They knew my husband. They knew who he was born, what happened. So he wrote them what happened, and they made us papers. And in no time, we got a visa to America. So my husband said, wherever we're going to have a chance to go first, we're going to go. So we had to go to Naples in order to get the visa. And the secretary who worked for the council used to take a lot of bribes. And she was so nice to us. My husband said he would buy her a box of chocolates. He didn't even think of what-- I said, don't you do that because you could get in trouble. So she never got nothing from us. And when we came on the boat here-- the people who came together, you know, different people from all different parts-- they ask how much did you give her? How much, $1,000 did you give. And they didn't believe we didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband said, see. I didn't even give her a-- you know, it was funny. But we came here, and the relatives were alive. I mean, they're all there. And they were very nice to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you get to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the month?

speaker: interviewee
text: In January.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my husband work for some people, you know, for a year. And we accumulated money. And then we went in business for ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a junk shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had my daughter. She was born here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shirley.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was she born? 1949. Yep. 1948. 1948. We came in January. She was born in August. So that's what it is. And when did you have your third child?

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reuben.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when my husband died, in 1979. Sold the business, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- you found out the details of what happened to your family before you left-- when you went to Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went from Rowne to [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't believe it because when I came back already, then I got a letter from them. And they were in a different town. And then I wrote them a letter, and they never got it. So we-- we-- I made papers for her, for Mrs. Blasberg. I don't remember. It was quite a few years. She had relatives in New York, but they wouldn't-- they wouldn't do it. But I made her papers and the Jewish Family Service. And I brought her here. And the other one went to Germany, and she got married. And she went to Israel with her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever gone back to Europe since you were here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I would never go there. That's-- no-- no-- that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came at the time, there was nothing. There was mountains of rubble from the whole city. Even they saw you-- when I was-- I was there may be a day and a half. So in the nighttime, I was with some gentiles. And they were staying with rifles because, you know, the Ukrainians used to kill. And it was dangerous. It was-- you couldn't walk nowhere. What could you do? And you've never had a desire to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I would never. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had no survivors of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. Nobody. From the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you ever have dreams about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think a lot of times. There's not a day I shouldn't think. And now that I'm retired, you know, there's not much to do. You lay nights, you don't sleep. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's hard. It's very hard, you know. Like I said, in one way I did survive, and I had a family. I had very nice children, educated. I have nice grandchildren. And the children respect me very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: But still it's that hurt inside. My mother was 38 years old. My father was 43. For what? Like the mother ask, why did they kill my kids? Why? Have you ever discussed your experiences with your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have they asked you about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I never make-- I try to forg-- you know-- to, to pass by it. You don't want--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't want-- you don't want what? You started to say you don't want.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Why hurt them? It's not a pleasant thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to cover that we did not cover in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You know, when we were travelling with that couple, we travelled-- went to Argentina. I showed you. We were very close with them. They were very educated people. They went to Argentina. She had a sister there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met these people in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Italy. Yeah. And we became very good friends. You know, not with everybody you could get close. And we were sitting on the railroad stations [?  ?] dirty. with the little-- she had a-- her little girl was, I think, a little older. Some gentiles-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was-- she was with some gentiles during the war. They were rich, and she could pay, and they kept her.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, you sit on the filthy railroad, and the soldiers look on you. And some throw you a chocolate. You know, at the time-- and, and you think you remember, you know. What could become from a person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or I remember laying the soldiers dead with a little bread. You couldn't do nothing to them. You didn't have nothing, even a little water to give. Like one soldier-- write to my-- he was from Czechoslovakia-- write my mother, and tell her I died here. He could talk, but he didn't survive. For hunger. They didn't-- he didn't have no [INAUDIBLE] for hunger [INAUDIBLE]. You know. And the river, it was a big river with blood-- red. It was in spring, early spring. It was ice and blood. You know, and you see people without hands, without-- I mean, I wasn't-- it was still better like in concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now 50--

speaker: interviewee
text: But--

speaker: interviewer
text: 50 Years later, what message or lesson do you have for your children, for your grandchildren, for the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm glad they're educated. And they-- would like they should be good to each other, not just to themselves, but in general, and do good things because we are in such a turmoil. Why couldn't people live in peace and respect each other? Why do we need wars and antisemitism? You know, you see--

speaker: interviewer
text: I could smell it. The people don't understand what-- me, who went through, could smell that, that filth, that injustice. Why? You ask why. And after all, we have one God. There is not a thousand gods. Even Jesus Christ was a Jew, too. And he didn't talk nothing bad. Why do they have to reverse him and make from him something what he was never? He was a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look at what they do. Now with Christmas, made up a, a holiday. You know, but us, in Poland, the last year, they used to kill Jews before Easter, because they said Jews have to have blood for Easter night. And it was the Church's fault, the Church's fault. I think the Church, the Catholic Church is very antisemitic. And they knew the truth. They knew the truth, but they don't teach the people the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. We thank you very much for letting us interview you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. I hope I helped something. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you I was not in concentration camp. So I never considered myself, you know-- But I still am glad. I still-- my husband was-- he was a workaholic always. And he-- but him, the children, he loved them so much. Because he lost-- you know, when they took away his wife and the child, the little boy said, mother, why doesn't daddy go with us?

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was always in front of him. And the children we had were so precious to him, until the last minute. He never let me say a, a loud word to them. If sometime I yell-- don't, don't yell at them. What you want? Leave them alone even.

speaker: interviewee
text: He let them go away with murder. Just, you know, so protective, very protective. And I understand why. You know, I understand why it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you again. We appreciate you sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: That in was Lodz, in a park. And he was six weeks old, my oldest son, Sheldon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Holding your oldest son Sheldon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was with a friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a friend. I don't remember their name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1945. --in Florence, in Italy. And Sheldon was nine months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's you on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband's name was Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been in 1945,

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Rome, in Castel Gandolfo. And he must have been about maybe a year and a half. Maybe a month, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's you and Sheldon, about a year and a half.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Sheldon. So-- because my daughter was born here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Milwaukee. And it must have been in 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: And left to right-- you're left.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm left, and then it's a cousin with her husband. And then it's my daughter Shirley. And my husband and Sheldon. This is a picture, I think, in my living room. I don't know. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say in '54.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband, and my daughter, and me, and my son, Sheldon.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me and my husband in the '60s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and my husband in the late '60s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Mark Plotkin, my son-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. You're starting in the back row--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I'm starting.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. On the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And my son, Reuben. And my daughter Shirley and her son, Gabrielle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was at his bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And that's me. And this is my older son, Sheldon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the first row on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Jennifer, mine oldest granddaughter. And then is Lyla, mine daughter's daughter. And then is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the other daughter from Sheldon. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Look at her. Yeah. I see-- then it's Reuben's wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 1987.

speaker: interviewee
text: '87. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the wedding of mine oldest son, Sheldon, in Denver in 1991. And the first is my son-in-law, Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then is my granddaughter, Jennifer. And then it's me and Sheldon with his bride.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's her name? I should remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it's my son, Reuben, with his daughter Michelle, and then my daughter Shirley, and my granddaughter Lyla, and my granddaughter Cirmel. See now I made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandson Joey, and he's 15 months old.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He's a puppy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my granddaughter, Michelle. And she's going to be four years old. And this is my daughter-in-law, Joanie, and my son Reuben.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for this opportunity to-- for recording this historical moment in our family, and I want to say a nice-- a few words about my mother. It's, it's, it's difficult to try to tell you what a strong woman she is. When I've told a story about my mother, how she was 15 years old, and one day instead of going home from school, she never saw her family again-- and, you know, her life on the run and trying to save her own life began-- when I tell people that story, and they try to imagine their own 15-year-old daughter being in school-- and saying, what if your daughter-- they came to her school-- and they said, you know, you have to run for the border. Your family is going to be destroyed. And-- It's almost incredible. And so that was 55 years ago. And from that point forward, this 15-year-old kid has become a woman of the world. I guess just in wrapping up, one of the proudest moments we've had is a culmination of that whole event-- is that my wife Joan, before we were married was Catholic. And I remember before we got married, one of the greatest moments of my life was at the conversion ceremony, when, in full view of the public, my wife got up and had to swear that regardless of what happened to me, that she wanted to be a Jew. And I thought, here's my mother on my left-- on my right. Then my new wife converting to Judaism on the left. And going from a point of running for her-- for her life from the Nazis because she was a Jew-- only because she was a Jew-- to this point where a woman would get up and publicly proclaim her desire to be a Jew. It was one of the most touching moments of my life. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, again, thank you very much. And God bless you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
